key_insight: This study shows that high-risk thrombotic primary antiphospholipid syndrome is marked by a complement-, interferon-, and platelet-driven innate immune programme that intensifies with thrombosis and antiphospholipid antibody burden.
clinical_relevance: The findings suggest blood-based molecular signatures could help stratify patients with thrPAPS into higher-risk subgroups, potentially informing closer monitoring and future biomarker-guided management research.
method: Whole-blood transcriptomics and plasma proteomics in thrPAPS patients vs age/sex-matched healthy controls
Differential expression, pathway enrichment, WGCNA, MOFA2, and network propagation to identify shared and latent programmes
Machine learning and longitudinal profiling to link signatures with recurrent events and disease activity
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that complement factor D discriminated recurrent from single-event thrPAPS with AUC 0.79 and correlated with thrombotic event count (Spearman rho 0.62, p < 0.001).
watch_item: These are biomarker-discovery results from a patient cohort, so the signals need external validation and prospective testing before being used in practice.
paper_title: Multi-omics and network propagation reveal latent innate immune programmes stratifying high-risk thrombotic primary antiphospholipid syndrome
